elements.sent-2411-001: The unique connectivity of kagome lattices gives rise to topological properties, such as flat bands and Dirac cones.
elements.sent-2411-002: When combined with ferromagnetism and a chemical potential near the 2D Dirac points, this structure offers the potential to realize the highly sought-after topological Chern magnetotransport.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recently, there was considerable excitement surrounding this possibility in the ferrimagnetic kagome metal TbMn$_\mathbf{6$Sn$_\mathbf{6}$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: However, density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the 2D Chern gap lies well above the Fermi energy, challenging its relevance in the observed anomalous Hall conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-005: Here, we investigate YMn$_\mathbf{6}$Sn$_\mathbf{5.45}$Ga$_\mathbf{0.55}$, a compound with similar crystallographic, magnetic, and electronic properties to TbMn$_\mathbf{6}$Sn$_\mathbf{6}$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings show that the intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity in this material, while comparable in magnitude to that in TbMn$_\mathbf{6}$Sn$_\mathbf{6}$, is fully three-dimensional, thus providing experimental evidence that Hall conductivity in this class of materials does not originate from 2D Chern gaps.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we confirm that the newly proposed empirical scaling relation for extrinsic Hall conductivity is universally governed by spin fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Kagome lattice magnets have attracted significant interest in condensed matter physics due to their high frustration in the case of antiferromagnetic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-009: Over the past decade, this interest has grown, as it has been shown that even unfrustrated ferromagnetic (or nonmagnetic) kagome planes can exhibit nontrivial electronic features, such as flat bands and Dirac cones \cite{zhang2011quantum,ghimire2020topology,kang2020dirac,ye2018massive,yin2019negative,kuroda2017evidence,superconductivity .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Recently, particular attention has been directed toward the so-called 166 family, the $R$Mn$_6$Sn$_6$ compounds, where $R$ represents a rare-earth element \cite{bolens2019topological,asaba2020anomalous,bhandari2024magnetism,pokharel2021electronic,jones2022origin,pokharel2022highly,arachchige2022charge,riberolles2023orbital,lee2022interplay .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In these compounds, Mn atoms form kagome planes, and the crystal structure provides a diverse material space for manipulating both electronic and magnetic properties (Fig. \ref{structure ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Compounds with non-magnetic $R$ atoms are simpler because magnetism arises solely from the Mn sublattice; however, they are also more complex due to frustrated interplanar magnetic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-013: As shown in Fig. \ref{structure , although all Mn planes are crystallographically equivalent, the exchange interactions are not; there are two distinct exchange paths: one ($J_1$) across the Sn layer and the other ($J_2$) across the $R$Sn layer.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ (Y166), a $R$166 compound with non-magnetic $R$ atom, $J_2 > 0$ (i.e., antiferromagnetic), while the dominant interaction is $J_1 < 0$.
elements.sent-2411-015: As in most metals, the exchange coupling decays relatively slowly with distance (roughly as 1/$d^3$), so the minimal model Hamiltonian includes $J_1$, $J_2$, and $J_3$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Notably, this Hamiltonian is frustrated if $J_2J_3 < 0$, resulting in intriguing spin-spiral orders that exhibit phenomena such as the topological Hall effect \cite{ghimire2020competing,wang2021field and Lifshitz transitions \cite{siegfried2022magnetization .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Fig1Ga.pdf} \caption{ Crystal structure. Sketch of the crystal structure of YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$. $J_i$s ($i=1,2,3)$ represent the exchange constants and $J_p$ represents the in-plane exchange constant. } \label{structure} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-017: Replacing Y with a magnetic ion that strongly couples to the neighboring Mn planes introduces an additional indirect ferromagnetic coupling between these planes, which can overcome the direct antiferromagnetic $J_2$ and remove magnetic frustration.
elements.sent-2411-018: This occurs in Tb166 \cite{riberolles2023orbital,yin2020quantum,riberolles2022low,mielke2022low,li2023discovery,xu2022topological,gao2021anomalous and is now well understood \cite{lee2022interplay,jones2022origin .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Additionally, due to well-understood reasons \cite{lee2022interplay , Tb has an easy axis anisotropy along the crystallographic $c$ direction, making the material a collinear easy-axis ferrimagnet at low temperatures (in contrast to Y166, which is an easy-plane spiral antiferromagnet).}
elements.sent-2411-020: Because of these specific characteristics, Tb166 recently garnered significant interest for various different properties \cite{yin2020quantum, jones2022origin,mielke2022low,xu2022topological,li2023discovery,Wenzel2022Effect including a putative two-dimensional (2D) Chern gap around 130 meV above the Fermi energy, as inferred from scanning tunneling measurements \cite{yin2020quantum .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: One of the consequences of a 2D Chern gap, when close to the Fermi energy, is large anomalous Hall effect.
elements.sent-2411-022: The intrinsic contribution to the anomalous Hall effect extracted from the scaling of the anomalous Hall resistivity matches the value expected from the Chern gap around 130 meV above the Fermi energy \cite{yin2020quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, density functional calculations \cite{jones2022origin,zhang2022exchange,lee2022interplay consistently have shown that the 2D-Chern gap in Tb166 lies about 700 meV above the Fermi energy and does not contribute to the anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC).}
elements.sent-2411-024: Instead, this AHC arises from several different regions in the Brillouin zone in ferrimagnetic Tb166 \cite{jones2022origin .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, an empirical scaling relation in the latter study suggested a potential contribution of spin fluctuations to the extrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity, an effect not previously investigated.
elements.sent-2411-026: Regarding the latter, the standard method based on the theory by Cr\'epieux and Bruno \cite{PhysRevB.64.014416 for extracting the intrinsic (related to the Berry phases of conducting electron) and extrinsic (due to electron scattering) contributions to the AHC is to fit the temperature dependence of the AHC to a simple scaling relation, \begin{equation}\sigma_{xy} = a\sigma^2_{xx}+ c \label{scaling0}\end{equation} where $c=\sigma^A_{xy}$ represents the intrinsic AHC, while the first term arises from defect scattering.}
elements.sent-2411-027: This scaling relation has been widely used to extract the intrinsic AHC in various materials \cite{Tian2009,ye2018massive,yin2020quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, Cr\'epieux and Bruno's derivation did not account for scattering from thermally excited spin fluctuations, and currently a theoretical analysis of the effect of spin fluctuations on AHC is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-029: Analyzing the Tb166 data, we found \cite{jones2022origin that Eq. \ref{scaling0 poorly describes $ \sigma_{xy}$ at higher temperatures, where scattering from spin fluctuations becomes increasingly important.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: At the same time, we found that the most fluctuating species in Tb166 is Tb, which fluctuates much stronger than Mn.
elements.sent-2411-031: We also found that the empirical formula, \begin{equation}\sigma_{xy} = a\sigma^2_{xx}+ d/\sigma_{xx}+c, \label{scaling1}\end{equation} fits the experimental data exceptionally well (note that the additional contribution becomes significant when $\sigma_{xx}$ is small, i.e., at higher temperatures).
elements.sent-2411-032: We tentatively attributed this term to spin fluctuations, primarily from Tb.
elements.sent-2411-033: A reliable protocol for extracting AHC from the experiment is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-034: The existing methods \cite{PhysRevB.64.014416,Tian2009,PhysRevB.96.144426 overlook spin fluctuations, and therefore rely on low-temperature data.}
elements.sent-2411-035: An equation that accurately describes the AHC across the entire temperature range is of significant practical importance.
elements.sent-2411-036: In this study, we address two key aspects concerning Tb166: first, whether the intrinsic contribution to the AHC is linked to the 2D Chern gap, and second, whether the component in the AHC scaling relation, attributed to spin fluctuations, can be observed in another $R$166 compound that does not contain Tb.
elements.sent-2411-037: This is particularly relevant as Tb was thought to be crucial for both the Chern-gap induced AHE \cite{yin2020quantum and the enhancement of spin fluctuations \cite{jones2022origin .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: An ideal compound to investigate these properties would be a $R$166 compound with a non-magnetic $R$ atom and a soft, and relatively isotropic ferromagnet.
elements.sent-2411-039: Such a compound would allow the access to the saturated ferromagnetic state in a standard laboratory setting, enabling measurements of anomalous Hall resistivity over a broad temperature range and in both in-plane and out-of-plane directions.
elements.sent-2411-040: We found that YMn$_6$Sn$_{6-x}$Ga$_x$, $0.30\leq x \leq 0.61$ meet these criteria \cite{xu2021magnetic and selected one particular composition YMn$_6$Sn$_{6.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ for the study.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Our results show that the intrinsic AHC in this compound is comparable to that in Tb166 for the out-of-plane magnetic field ($B$), where the Chern gap is expected to contribute to the AHC.
elements.sent-2411-042: At the same time, we observed a similar AHC in the in-plane $B$, where the Chern gap contribution is not expected, confirming the 3D nature of the AHC.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, we verified the new AHC scaling introduced in Ref. \cite{jones2022origin and confirmed its spin-fluctuational origin.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Single crystals of YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ were grown by using Sn as a flux by the molten flux method.
elements.sent-2411-045: Y pieces (Alfa Aesar 99.9\%), Mn pieces (Alfa Aesar 99.95\%), Sn shots (Alfa Aesar 99.999\%) and Ga pieces (Alfa Aesar 99.9999\%) were added into a 2-ml aluminum oxide crucible in molar ratio of 1:6:18:2.
elements.sent-2411-046: The crucible was then sealed in a fused silica ampule under vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-047: The sealed ampule was heated to 1150 $^{\circ}$C over 10 hours, homogenized at 1150 $^{\circ}$C for 24 hours, and then cooled down to 600 $^{\circ}$C with a rate of 5 $^{\circ}$C/h.
elements.sent-2411-048: Once the furnace reached 600 $^{\circ}$C, the molten flux was separated from the crystals by using a centrifuge.
elements.sent-2411-049: Upon opening the crucible, nice hexagonal-looking crystals up to 20 mg were obtained.
elements.sent-2411-050: The crystal structure and the atomic composition were verified from a single crystal X-ray diffraction experiment.
elements.sent-2411-051: An arbitrary sphere of data was collected on a silver block-like crystal, having appropriate dimensions of $0.073\times0.068\times0.026$, on a Bruker D8 diffractometer equipped with a Bruker APEX-II detector using a combination of $\omega$- and $\phi$-scans of 0.5 degree \cite{apex4 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Data were corrected for absorption and polarization effects and analyzed for space group determination \cite{krause2015comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The structure was solved by dual-space methods and expanded routinely \cite{sheldrick2015shelxt .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The model was refined by full-matrix least-squares analysis of F$^2$ against all reflections \cite{sheldrick2015crystal .}
elements.sent-2411-055: All non-hydrogen atoms were refined with anisotropic atomic displacement parameters.
elements.sent-2411-056: Unless otherwise noted, hydrogen atoms were included in calculated positions.
elements.sent-2411-057: Atomic displacement parameters for the hydrogen were tied to the equivalent isotropic displacement parameter of the atom to which they are bonded ( $U_{iso (H) =1.5 U_{eq (C)$ for methyl, $1.2 U_{eq (C)$ for all others.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: DC magnetization, resistivity, and magnetoresistance measurements were performed in a Quantum Design Dynacool Physical Property Measurement System (PPMS) with a 9 T magnet.
elements.sent-2411-059: ACMS II option was used in the same PPMS for DC magnetization measurements.
elements.sent-2411-060: Single crystals of YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ were polished to adequate dimensions for electrical transport measurements.
elements.sent-2411-061: Crystals were oriented with the [001] and [100] directions parallel to the applied field for the c-axis and ab-plane measurements.
elements.sent-2411-062: Resistivity and Hall measurements were done using the 4-probe method.
elements.sent-2411-063: Pt wires of 25 $\mu$m were used for electrical contacts with contact resistances $<$ 30 $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Contacts were affixed with Epotek H20E silver epoxy.
elements.sent-2411-065: An electric current of 4 mA was used for the electrical transport measurements.
elements.sent-2411-066: Contact misalignment in the Hall resistivity measurement was corrected by anti-symmetrizing the measured data in positive and negative magnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-067: The first-principles calculations were performed using Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) \cite{Kresse1996 within projector augmented wave (PAW) method \cite{blochl1994,Kresse1999 The Perdew-Burke-Enzerhof (PBE) \cite{PBE generalized gradient approximation was employed to describe exchange-correlation effects.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The on-site Coulomb interactions are taken into account using LDA+U \cite{Anisimov1991 to improve the description of the interactions between localized $3d$-electrons of Mn and an effective $U_{eff =U-J=0$, $0.6$ and $2$ are considered.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The experimental values were used for the lattice parameters and kept fixed for all the calculations, including the geometry optimization, where only internal coordinates were relaxed.
elements.sent-2411-070: To properly determine the structure, we performed geometry optimization for $2d$, $2e$, and $2c$ three different Ga substitution sites.
elements.sent-2411-071: The 2c site, consistent with the experimental analysis, gives the lowest energy which is then considered as the magnetic ground state.
elements.sent-2411-072: The crystal structure and atomic composition of YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ (Y166-Ga) were determined using single crystal X-ray diffraction.
elements.sent-2411-073: Similar to the parent compound YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ (Y166), Y166-Ga adopts a hexagonal $P6/mmm$ structure $( a = b = 5.4784$\ \AA, $c = 8.925$\ \AA), consisting of kagome planes [Mn$_3$Sn] separated by two inequivalent layers Sn$_2$ and Sn$_3$YGa, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{structure .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The structure exhibits Sn-site doping with Ga, specifically at the Sn3 site (Wyckoff position 2c).
elements.sent-2411-075: This was confirmed through modeling efforts for partial Ga occupancy at other Sn sites, which either resulted in poorer fits to the data or nonsensical Ga occupancy values.
elements.sent-2411-076: Refinement analysis determined the Sn:Ga site occupancy to be 0.725:0.175, corresponding to the full chemical formula noted above, i.e., $\sim$ 0.55 Ga atoms per unit cell.
elements.sent-2411-077: Detailed crystallographic parameters from the single crystal X-ray diffraction experiment are summarized in Table \ref{T1 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Our DFT calculations, presented in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-079: YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ can thus be viewed as a moderately hole-doped ($\approx 0.09$ h/Mn) derivative of the parent compound Y166.

elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Fig2suscGa.pdf} \caption{ \textbf{Magnetic properties of YMn$_\mathbf{6}$Sn$_\mathbf{5.45}$Ga$_\mathbf{0.55}$.} (a) Magnetic susceptibility measured under an external magnetic field $B$ of 0.1 T along the [100] and [001] directions using the field-cooled protocol. (b-c) Magnetization as a function of magnetic field $B||$ [100] (b) and $B||$ [001] (c) at selected temperatures ranging from 1.8 to 300 K.} \label{susc} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-080: The temperature dependence of magnetic susceptibility ($\chi = M/B$) measured with a magnetic field $B$ of 0.1 T parallel to [100] ($\chi_{100}$) and along [001] ($\chi_{001}$) is shown in Fig \ref{susc (a).}
elements.sent-2411-081: These susceptibility data indicate that Y166-Ga undergoes a paramagnetic-to-ferromagnetic ordering below 350 K, consistent with previous reports \cite{xu2021magnetic, zhang2001magnetism .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Additionally, the easy-plane behavior is evident from the significantly larger $\chi_{100}$ compared to $\chi_{001}$ below the transition temperature ($T_c$), with an anisotropy ratio of $\chi_{100 /\chi_{001 = 5$ just below $T_c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: It is to be noted that parent compound Y166 orders with a commensurate antiferromagnetic helical structure below 345 K and exhibits an incommensurate double helical structure (DH) upon further cooling \cite{ghimire2020competing,siegfried2022magnetization,dally2021chiral , while YMn$_6$Sn$_{1-x}$Ga$_{x}$ compounds show a ferromagnetic transition for doping concentrations $x>0.30$ \cite{xu2021magnetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Figures \ref{susc (b) and (c) show the isothermal magnetization curves of Y166-Ga at some representative temperatures for $B$ $||$ [100] ($M_{100}$) and [001] ($M_{001}$), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-085: In the entire temperature range measured $M_{100}$ saturates below 0.5 T with a negligible hysteresis, while $M_{001}$ saturates at slightly larger $B$.
elements.sent-2411-086: At 1.8 K $M_{001}$ saturates at 2.2 T, which decreases with increasing temperature (1.7 T at 300 K), also with negligibly small hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-087: At 1.8 K, saturation magnetization ($M_{sat}$) along [100] is 9.85 $\mu\begin{equation}_B$/f.u. while it is 10.7 $\mu\end{equation}_B$/f.u along [001].
elements.sent-2411-088: In either direction, $M_{sat}$ gradually decreases with the increase in temperature, which attains a value of 7.5 $\mu\begin{equation}_B$/f.u. along [100] and 7.7 $\mu\end{equation}_B$/f.u along [001].
elements.sent-2411-089: The ratio of the saturated magnetization at 1.8 to 300 K ($M_{sat,1.8K}$/ $M_{sat,300K}$) is 1.39 along the [001] direction and 1.31 along the [100] direction.
elements.sent-2411-090: For Tb166, the Mn moment shows the ratio of $\sim$ 1.05 and the Tb moment exhibits the ratio of around 1.66 \cite{Idrissi1991, jones2022origin .}
elements.sent-2411-091: This suggests that the Mn moments in Y166-Ga experience more fluctuation between 1.8 and 300 K than in Tb166, where the Tb moments are the most fluctuating ones \cite{jones2022origin .}
elements.sent-2411-092: This observation is consistent with the expectation, as the Curie temperature of Tb166 is about 70 K higher than that of Y166-Ga.
elements.figure-2411-004: \renewcommand*{\arraystretch}{1.4} \begin{tabular}{ccccc} \multicolumn{3}{c}{$x=1$} $x=0$ (Y166)\tabularnewline U 0 0.6 2 0.6 \tabularnewline $J_{1}$ -14.5 -11.9 -17.3 -12.86 \tabularnewline $J_{2}$ -21.7 -10.6 -21.9 4.66 \tabularnewline $J_{3}$ 1.8 1.5 -4.0 -2.20 \tabularnewline \end{tabular} \caption{\label{tab:jex} Calculated exchange couplings $J_1$, $J_2$ and $J_3$ in unit of meV. The data for Y166 are taken from Ref. \cite{ghimire2020competing}.}
elements.sent-2411-093: To understand the doping-induced phase transition, we adopt the $J_1-J_3$ effective model proposed in Ref. \cite{ROSENFELD20081898 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: The spin Hamiltonian is expressed as follows \begin{equation}H =\sum_{\left\langle ij\right\rangle _{1}}J_{1}S_{i}S_{j} +\sum_{\left\langle ij\right\rangle _{2}}J_{2}S_{i}S_{j} +\sum_{\left\langle ij\right\rangle _{3}}J_{3}S_{i}S_{j} \label{eq:spinham}\end{equation} where $J_{i}$ ($i=1-3)$ are the exchange interaction parameters as indicated in Fig. \ref{structure .}
elements.sent-2411-095: The parameters were extracted using the least squared fitting of our DFT calculations into Eq. \ref{eq:spinham .}
elements.sent-2411-096: According to a thorough DFT-based analysis of the phase diagram for parent Y166 discussed in our earlier work \cite{ghimire2020competing , we observed that $U_{eff =0.6$ best reproduce the magnetic states for parent Y166 observed in the experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, in our study, we considered the same $U$ parameter with one additional larger $U=2$ for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-098: The results along with those of Y166, taken from Ref. \cite{ghimire2020competing , are summarized in TABLE \ref{tab:jex .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The fitting is achieved with excellent quality in all three cases, as all the relative energy differences between different magnetic states can be consistently and accurately reproduced by the model, especially in the case of $U=0.6$ where the average error is less than 1 meV.
elements.sent-2411-100: This suggests that the minimal model adopted here is appropriate and reliable.
elements.sent-2411-101: In TABLE \ref{tab:jex , one can see that while $J_{1}$ and $J_{2}$ consistently favor ferromagnetic alignment regardless of different U values, the Hubbard U correction tends to stabilize the FM states further as $J_{3}$ shifts from positive to negative as U increases.}
elements.sent-2411-102: According to the analytically determined phase diagram Ref. \cite{ROSENFELD20081898 the spin model for all three U values yields the same correct FM ground state.}
elements.sent-2411-103: To assess the doping effect, we compare the results of the two compounds for the same $U=0.6$ (i.e. columns 3 and 5).
elements.sent-2411-104: In Y166, $J_{1}$ dominates and has the same (opposite) sign as $J_{3}$ ($J_{2}$).
elements.sent-2411-105: This arrangement leads to a competition between $J_{2}$ and $J_{3}$ which results in the formation of a helical magnetic state \cite{ghimire2020competing,ROSENFELD20081898 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: However, with Ga-doping, $J_{2}$ becomes ferromagnetic and now comparable to $J_{1}$ in strength.
elements.sent-2411-107: Although $J_{3}$ becomes antiferromagnetic, it is too weak to induce frustration.
elements.sent-2411-108: This qualitative shift aligns with expectations, considering the direct alteration of exchange pathways for $J_{2}$ and $J_{3}$ induced by the presence of doped-Ga.
elements.sent-2411-109: As a consequence of these changes, the frustration that was initially developed in the pure Y166 to promote the helical magnetic state is effectively mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-110: The system undergoes a transition, and the magnetic state collapses into a ferromagnetic (FM) order.
elements.sent-2411-111: It is interesting to note that while all configurations predict the same correct ferromagnetic ground state, only $U=2$ gives the correct easy-plane anisotropy and $U=0$ and $0.6$ give very small easy-axis anisotropy.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Fig3RhoGa.pdf} \caption{ \textbf{Electrical transport properties of YMn$_\mathbf{6}$Sn$_\mathbf{5.45}$Ga$_\mathbf{0.55}$.} (a) Electrical resistivity as a function of temperature for current applied along the [100] and [001] directions. (b) Ratio of the conductivity along the [001] direction to that along [100]. (c) Angular magnetoresistance (AMR) as a function of angle $\theta$, measured under a 9 T magnetic field, where the current ($I$) is along the [100] direction and $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field and [120] direction, as illustrated by the sketch in panel (c).} \label{res} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-112: The temperature dependence of electrical resistivity of Y166-Ga, measured with the electric current applied along the [100] direction ($\rho_{100}$, blue curve) and the [001] direction ($\rho_{001}$, red curve) over the temperature range 1.8-400 K, is shown in Fig. \ref{res (a).}
elements.sent-2411-113: The resistivity decreases as temperature decreases, indicating the metallic behavior of the sample.
elements.sent-2411-114: Residual resistivity ratio (RRR), calculated as $\rho(400 K)/\rho(2 K)$, is 9 for $I||[100]$ and is 18 for $I||[001]$.
elements.sent-2411-115: These values are smaller than those in Y166 \cite{ghimire2020competing,siegfried2022magnetization , likely due to disorder induced by doping.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Both $\rho_{100}$ and $\rho_{001}$ exhibit a kink at 350 K, indicative of the onset of a ferromagnetic transition, as observed in the susceptibility measurements [Fig. \ref{susc (a)].}
elements.sent-2411-117: Across the entire temperature range, $\rho_{100}$ is greater than $\rho_{001}$.
elements.sent-2411-118: The conductivity anisotropy, $\sigma_{[001] /\sigma_{[100]}$, is plotted in Fig. \ref{res (b), showing a value $>$ 2, which suggests that the electronic transport in Y166-Ga is three dimensional.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: This behavior differs from that of the parent compound Y166, where in-plane conductivity is greater than the out-of-plane conductivity \cite{siegfried2022magnetization ).}
elements.sent-2411-120: The enhanced $c$-axis conductivity observed in Y166-Ga is similar to that found in Ge doped YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ \cite{bhandari2024magnetism .}
elements.sent-2411-121: The anisotropic transport behavior was further investigated by measuring the angular-dependent magnetoresistance (AMR).
elements.sent-2411-122: In this measurement, an electric current $I$ was applied along the [100] direction, while the sample was rotated around the magnetic field within the crystallographic $bc$-plane, with $I\bot B$ held constant so that only transverse MR was measured.
elements.sent-2411-124: Since the largest magnetic saturation field is below 2.5 T (see Fig. 2), the 9T magnetic field aligns the magnetic moment $M$ perpendicular to $I$ at all times.
elements.sent-2411-125: The AMR, defined as [\{$\rho_{xx (\theta)-\rho_{xx (\theta=0)\}/\rho_{xx (\theta=0)]\times100$, measured at 5 K is shown in Fig. \ref{res (c), with maximum value of $-5.6\%$, indicating a substantial effect.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: To gain further insight into this large AMR, we compared the AMR with the ab initio calculated AMR using the GGA+U method.
elements.sent-2411-127: For this purpose, we used the all-electron WIEN2k package \cite{WIEN , varying $U_{eff =U-J$ from 1.2 to 2 eV.}}

elements.sent-2411-129: We calculated this quantity using the $19\times19\times11$ zone-centered k-point mesh and tetrahedron numerical integration, and an otherwise default setup.

elements.sent-2411-133: Hall resistivity measured with with $B||[001]$ and the current $I||[100]$ ($\rho_{yx}$) at representative temperatures is shown in Fig. \ref{hall (a).}
elements.sent-2411-134: The zero-field value of $\rho_{yx}$ corresponds to the anomalous Hall resistivity $\rho_{yx ^{A}$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: In Fig. \ref{hall (b), we show the longitudinal Hall conductivity, $\sigma_{xx}$ = $1/\rho_{xx}$, and the absolute value of the anomalous Hall conductivity, $\sigma_{yx ^{A}$ = $-\rho_{yx /\rho_{xx}$ (valid when $\rho_{yx}$ $\ll$ $\rho_{xx ^2$, and $\rho_{xx}$ = $\rho_{yy}$), as a function of temperature between 1.8 and 300 K.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: This clearly indicates that $\sigma_{yx ^{A}$ varies with temperature across the entire temperature range.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The scaling of the $\sigma_{yx ^{A}$, using the relation presented in Eq. \ref{scaling1 , is shown in Fig. \ref{hall (c).}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: This scaling law effectively fits the high-temperature data, yielding the coefficients $a$, $d$, and $c$, as presented in Table \ref{scaling and compared to those from Tb166.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The coefficient $a$, representing impurity scattering, is about an order of magnitude larger in Y166-Ga compared to Tb166, as expected due to increased disorder scattering in the doped sample.
elements.sent-2411-140: The intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity of Y166-Ga (121 S/cm) is comparable to that of Tb166 (140 S/cm).
elements.sent-2411-141: Interestingly, the magnitude of $d$ is smaller in the Y166-Ga than in Tb166, consistent with the hypothesis that the term $d/\sigma_{xx}$ in Eq. \ref{scaling1 is due to spin fluctuations \cite{jones2022origin .}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Notably, $d\neq 0$ in Y166-Ga despite the absence of highly fluctuating Tb atoms, likely because, as discussed in Section \ref{sec:3b , Mn in Y166-Ga fluctuates significantly more than in Tb166.}
elements.sent-2411-143: This implies that the $d/\sigma_{xx}$ is essential in the anomalous Hall scaling of $R$Mn$_6$Sn$_6$ compounds due to the presence of Mermin-Wagner fluctuations \cite{ghimire2020competing involving either Mn or $R$ atoms.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Comparison of intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity between TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ \cite{jones2022origin} and YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$.} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ |c|c|c|c| } Compound $c$ (S/cm) $a$ (S/cm)$^{-1}$ $d$ (S/cm)$^2$ TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ ($|\sigma_{yx}|$) 140 $8.69 \times 10^{-9}$ $-2.47 \times 10^{5}$ YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ ($|\sigma_{yx}|$) 121 3.25 $\times 10^{-8}$ $-1.75 \times 10^{5} $ YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ ($|\sigma_{xz}|$) 310 $1.89\times 10^{-7}$ $-5.41 \times 10^{5}$ \end{tabular} } \label{scaling}
elements.sent-2411-144: The similar magnitude of intrinsic AHC in Y166-Ga and Tb166 suggests that the intrinsic AHC in $R$166 compounds is a general property of ferrimagnetism rather than a result of exotic Chern physics, consistent with the conclusion in Ref. \cite{jones2022origin .}
elements.sent-2411-145: This interpretation is further supported by photoemission spectroscopy data from the parent compound Y166 \cite{li2021dirac , which shows no such topological feature above the Fermi energy, with Ga doping shifting the Fermi energy even lower.}
elements.sent-2411-146: To further validate this interpretation, we measured the Hall conductivity of Y166-Ga by applying an in-plane magnetic field, ensuring that any 2D Chern gap contributions would be excluded if present.
elements.sent-2411-147: While measuring this in-plane configuration for Tb166 would require an exceptionally high magnetic field due to magnetic saturation constraints at low temperatures \cite{kimura2006high,jones2022origin , Y166-Ga can be measured under standard lab conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-148: For consistency with the $\rho_{yx}$ measurement in Fig. \ref{hall , we applied the current along the [100] direction and applied the in-plane field along [120].}
elements.sent-2411-149: The Hall resistivity, $\rho_{zx}$, measured across a range of temperatures from 1.8 K to 300 K, is shown in Fig. \ref{hall1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-150: In Fig. \ref{hall1 (b), we plot $\sigma_{xx}$ = 1/$\rho_{xx}$ alongside the absolute value of the anomalous Hall conductivity, $\sigma_{zx}$ = $-\rho_{zx /(\rho_{xx}\rho_{zz )$, which is valid when $\rho_{zx}$ $\ll$ $\rho_{xx}$ and $\rho_{zz}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: The scaling of $\sigma_{zx}$, following the relation in Eq. \ref{scaling1 , is presented in Fig. \ref{hall1 (c), with the fitting coefficients $a$, $d$, and $c$ presented in Table \ref{scaling .}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, the intrinsic AHC contribution, represented by coefficient $c$ ($\sigma^{A _{zx,int}$), is significantly larger than $\sigma^{A _{yx,int}$, indicating that the AHC in Y166-Ga has a 3D nature and can be large without invoking the Chern physics.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: The coefficient $|d|$ is also larger in this measurement geometry, potentially pointing to enhanced spin-fluctuations, though this cannot be directly compared to Tb166 due to differing Hall geometries and remains a topic of future work on $R$166 compounds.
elements.sent-2411-154: However, it is noteworthy that the $d/\sigma_{xx}$ term is also necessary in this case.
elements.sent-2411-155: Nevertheless, our findings reveal that the AHC in Y166-Ga displays 3D characteristics and supports theoretical calculations that suggest the AHC arises from predominantly 3D bands \cite{jones2022origin , as further discussed in Section \ref{sec:3c .}}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Intrinsic AHC calculations for TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ and YMn$_6$Sn$_6$
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Fig7Ga.pdf} \caption{ Primitive unit cell used in AHC calculations. The top view of the YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ primitive unit cell. The $x$- and $y$-axes are highlighted in red, while the [120] direction is denoted in blue. The $z$-axis is perpendicular to the plane.} \label{structure2} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth,clip]{AHCTbMn6Sn6outofplane1.pdf} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth,clip]{AHCYMn6Sn6outofplane1.pdf} \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth,clip]{AHCYMn6Sn6inplane1.pdf} \end{tabular} \caption{ \textbf{Calculated intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity (AHC) of TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ and YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ as a function of Fermi energy ($\boldsymbol{E}_{\text{F}}$).} The intrinsic AHC of TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ (Top) was calculated with an out-of-plane magnetization configuration, while that of YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ was calculated with both out-of-plane magnetization (Middle) and in-plane magnetization configurations (Bottom).} \label{ymn6sn6_ahc}
elements.sent-2411-156: The intrinsic AHC can be calculated by integrating the Berry curvature over the Brillouin zone (BZ)~\cite{wang2006ab : \begin{eqnarray}\label{ahc:theo} \sigma_{\alpha\beta}=-\dfrac{e^2}{\hbar} \int_\text{BZ}\dfrac{d\vec{k}}{(2\pi)^3}\sum_{n} f(E_{n\vec{k}}) \Omega_{n}(\vec{k})\,\end{eqnarray} where $f(E_{n\vec{k )$ is the Fermi-Dirac distribution, $\Omega_{n,\alpha\beta (\vec{k )$ is the contribution to the Berry curvature from state $n$, and $\alpha,\beta = \{x,y,z\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: A notable aspect of the calculation is the pronounced and rapid oscillation observed in the Berry curvature across the BZ, requiring a dense $k$ mesh to ensure convergence.
elements.sent-2411-158: To accelerate the calculations, we implemented Eq. \ref{ahc:theo in our recently-developed tight-binding (TB) code~\cite{ke2019intersublattice and carried out the AHC calculations in $R$Mn$_6$Sn${_6}$ where $R$ = Tb and Y.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: A realistic TB Hamiltonian was constructed using the maximally localized Wannier functions (MLWFs) method~\cite{marzari1997maximally, souza2001maximally, marzari2012maximally implemented in Wannier90~\cite{mostofi2014updated after the self-consistent density-functional-theory calculations performed using Wien2k.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: A set of 118 Wannier functions (WFs) consisting of Y-$4d$ (or Tb-$5d$), Mn-$3d$, and Sn-$sp$ orbitals offers an effective representation of the electronic structure near the Fermi level ($E_{\text{F }$).}
elements.sent-2411-161: The self-consistent DFT calculations were carried out with out-of-plane magnetization in TbMn$_6$Sn${_6}$ and both in- and out-of-plane magnetization in YMn$_6$Sn${_6}$.
elements.sent-2411-162: For the in-plane YMn$_6$Sn${_6}$ configuration, the moment is along lattice vector $\mathbf{a}$, as denoted in Fig.\ref{structure2 .}
elements.sent-2411-163: A dense $256^3$ $k$-point mesh is used for the AHC calculations in TB.
elements.sent-2411-164: Figure~\ref{structure2 presents the unit cell of YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ used in our calculation.}
elements.sent-2411-165: The Cartesian coordinate system is chosen so that the lattice vector $\mathbf{b}$ is along the $y$-axis, $\mathbf{c}$ is along the $z$-axis, and $\mathbf{a}$ is along the $-30^\circ$ direction off the $x$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-166: Lattice vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ point along the nearest neighboring (NN) Mn-Mn bond direction.
elements.sent-2411-167: For the $\sigma_{xy}$ calculations discussed below, the first subscript $x$ denotes the current direction, and the second subscript $y$ denotes the Hall-field direction.
elements.sent-2411-168: Figure~\ref{ymn6sn6_ahc shows the AHC values calculated at $T=0$~K as functions of Fermi energy using Eq.~\ref{ahc:theo .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: In the out-of-plane orientation of both TbMn$_6$Sn$_6$ and YMn$_6$Sn$_6$, only $\sigma_{xy}$ exhibits substantial values, while $\sigma_{yz}$ and $\sigma_{zx}$ remain negligible, as illustrated in the top and middle panels of Figure~\ref{ymn6sn6_ahc .}
elements.sent-2411-170: Conversely, with in-plane magnetization in YMn$_6$Sn$_6$, both $\sigma_{yz}$ and $\sigma_{zx}$ demonstrate appreciable values, while $\sigma_{xy}$ remains negligible, as depicted in the bottom panel of Figure~\ref{ymn6sn6_ahc .}
elements.sent-2411-171: The band-filling calculation indicates that when doping YMn$_6$Sn$_6$ by $0.09$ hole/Mn, corresponding to a Fermi energy shift of -0.037 eV, $\sigma_{xy =77.62$ S/cm, and $\sigma_{zx =36.76$ S/cm.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: While the former closely aligns with the experimental value, the latter is approximately 10 times smaller than the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-173: These comparison has to be taken with caution, as random substitution of 0.55 Ga likely affects the electronic structure beyond a simple rigid Fermi energy shift.
elements.sent-2411-174: Nevertheless, our theoretical calculations predict that the Hall transport in $R$166 is 3D, consistent with the experimental findings.
elements.sent-2411-175: We reported results of magnetic and electrical transport measurements of YMn$_6$Sn$_{5.45}$Ga$_{0.55}$ in two different geometries, supported by first-principles and DFT calculations.
elements.sent-2411-176: Our magneto-transport measurements across these two geometries confirm a more reliable scaling law that not only extracts the intrinsic AHC, but also accounts for contributions from spin fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-177: These measurements revealed the 3D nature of the intrinsic AHC, which we attribute to the ferromagnetic properties of the material.
elements.sent-2411-178: The excellent agreement of the AHE with the empirical scaling law over the entire temperature suggests that this scaling relation may be important for not only for the large family of $R$Mn$_6$Sn$_6$ ferro/ferrimagnetic compounds, but also for other systems with strong spin fluctuations.
